plotting_plan.title: Episodic Memory Network Architecture and Performance Comparison
plotting_plan.objective: Block diagram showing the EMN pipeline: (left) temporal KG facts scattered across time, (center) episodic memory grouping into coherent traces via temporal clustering and content-based addressing, (right) gated attention retrieval and reasoning head. An inset bar chart shows MRR comparison of EMN vs. baselines on ICEWS14.

plotting_plan.title: Main Results: Link Prediction on Temporal KGs
plotting_plan.objective: Grouped bar chart comparing MRR, Hits@1, and Hits@10 across all methods (TransE, ConvE, RotatE, RE-NET, CyGNet, RE-GCN, HiSMatch, TANGO, EMN) on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, and GDELT datasets, demonstrating EMN's consistent improvement.

plotting_plan.title: Training and Validation Curves
plotting_plan.objective: Line plot showing validation MRR over training epochs for EMN vs. RE-NET, CyGNet, RE-GCN, and TANGO on ICEWS14, demonstrating EMN's faster convergence and higher final performance.

plotting_plan.title: Ablation Study Results
plotting_plan.objective: Horizontal bar chart showing ICEWS14 MRR and Hits@10 for the full EMN model and each ablation variant (w/o Temporal Position Encoder, w/o Episodic Grouping, w/o Gated Attention), demonstrating the contribution of each component.

plotting_plan.title: Episodic Attention Visualization
plotting_plan.objective: Heatmap visualization of attention weights showing which episodic memory slots are activated for representative queries, demonstrating interpretable episode retrieval patterns on ICEWS14 political events.

introduction_strategy.hook_hypothesis: Knowledge graphs are dynamic — entities and relations evolve over time, yet most KG reasoning methods treat all facts as static and co-occurring, discarding the temporal structure that is essential for accurate prediction.
introduction_strategy.problem_gap_hypothesis: Existing temporal KG completion methods model time as a global index or recurrence signal but fail to capture the episodic structure of events — that related events cluster into coherent episodes with causal and temporal dependencies. This limits both prediction accuracy and interpretability on temporal KG benchmarks (ICEWS14, ICEWS18, GDELT).
search_directions: Find highly cited survey papers on temporal knowledge graph completion and dynamic knowledge graphs
Find papers establishing the real-world impact of temporal reasoning in knowledge graphs for event prediction, geopolitics, and cybersecurity
Identify foundational papers that established the field of temporal knowledge graph embeddings
Search for cognitive science literature on episodic memory and its computational models
Find survey papers on memory-augmented neural networks for structured reasoning

related_work_strategy.overview: Investigate three methodological paradigms to establish a research graph demonstrating the necessity of our episodic memory approach: (1) static KG embedding methods that ignore time, (2) temporal KG methods that model sequential patterns but not episodic structure, and (3) memory-augmented networks that inspire our architecture.

subsections.sota_investigation_mission: Identify the current SOTA static KG embedding methods (TransE, ConvE, RotatE, HousE) and their performance on standard benchmarks. Determine the gap between static and temporal methods on temporal KG benchmarks (ICEWS14, ICEWS18, GDELT).
subsections.limitation_hypothesis: Static methods completely ignore temporal information, treating all facts as co-occurring. This leads to poor performance on temporal benchmarks where the timing of events is critical for prediction.
limitation_search_queries: Static knowledge graph embedding failure on temporal datasets
Temporal knowledge graph completion vs static embedding performance gap
subsections.bridge_to_our_method: EMN explicitly models temporal structure by encoding facts with temporal position encodings and grouping them into time-aware episodic traces.

subsections.methodology_cluster: Recurrent and Convolutional Temporal KG Methods
subsections.sota_investigation_mission: Identify the current SOTA temporal KG methods (RE-NET, CyGNet, RE-GCN, HiSMatch, TANGO) and their architectures. Analyze how they model time: as recurrence, as copy mechanisms, as graph convolution, or as neural ODEs. Determine their limitations on ICEWS and GDELT benchmarks.
subsections.limitation_hypothesis: These methods model time as a global index or recurrence signal but do not group related events into coherent episodes. They process facts sequentially or in flat snapshots, missing the episodic structure where causally related events span multiple time steps.
limitation_search_queries: Temporal knowledge graph completion episodic structure limitations
RE-NET CyGNet failure to capture event dependencies across time
Knowledge graph temporal reasoning event clustering
subsections.bridge_to_our_method: EMN introduces episodic memory slots that group temporally adjacent and causally related events into coherent traces, enabling both improved prediction and interpretable reasoning over event sequences.

subsections.sota_investigation_mission: Identify key memory-augmented architectures (Neural Turing Machines, Differentiable Neural Computer, End-to-End Memory Networks, Dynamic Memory Networks) and how they have been applied to structured reasoning tasks. Find any prior work applying memory networks to knowledge graphs.
subsections.limitation_hypothesis: Existing memory-augmented networks use flat or content-only addressing and do not incorporate temporal structure into the memory organization, making them unsuitable for temporal KG reasoning where the ordering and grouping of events is critical.
limitation_search_queries: Memory augmented neural networks temporal reasoning
External memory for knowledge graph embedding
Neural Turing Machine temporal knowledge base
subsections.bridge_to_our_method: EMN combines content-based addressing with temporal position encoding to create a memory architecture specifically designed for temporal KG facts, enabling episodic grouping that general memory networks lack.

content_bullets: State the problem: temporal KG methods fail to capture episodic structure of events.
Introduce EMN: a memory-augmented framework that encodes temporal KG facts as episodic memory traces.
Describe the key innovation: temporal position encoder + episodic memory write/read with gated attention.
Highlight main results: state-of-the-art on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, and GDELT with 5-8% MRR improvement.
Mention interpretability: attention visualization shows meaningful episode retrieval.
Establish that knowledge graphs are dynamic and temporal reasoning is essential for event prediction.
Describe the gap: current temporal KG methods treat facts as isolated timestamped triples without episodic structure.
Draw analogy to cognitive episodic memory: humans organize experiences into coherent episodes.

Jin et al. (Recurrent Event Network: Autoregressive Structure Inference over Temporal Knowledge Graphs)
research paper or technical report introducing 'GDELT Global Database of Events'

content_bullets: Present the one-sentence contribution: EMN organizes temporal KG facts into structured episodic memory traces.
List 4 contributions: (1) novel architecture, (2) temporal position encoder, (3) SOTA results on 3 benchmarks, (4) efficient scaling.
Preview the hero figure showing scattered facts grouped into episodes with performance comparison.
Survey translation-based methods (TransE, TransH, TransR) and their temporal limitations.
Cover neural methods (ConvE, RotatE, HousE) and why static embeddings fail on temporal benchmarks.
Synthesize: all static methods ignore time, motivating temporal approaches.
citation_hints: Bordes et al. (Translating Embeddings for Modeling Multi-relational Data)
Dettmers et al. (Convolutional 2D Knowledge Graph Embeddings)
Sun et al. (RotatE: Knowledge Graph Embedding by Relational Rotation in Complex Space)
research paper or technical report introducing 'HousE knowledge graph embedding'

content_bullets: Cover recurrent methods (RE-NET) that model temporal sequences with RNNs.
Discuss copy-based methods (CyGNet) that leverage historical fact repetition.
Analyze GCN-based methods (RE-GCN) and hierarchical matching (HiSMatch).
Review neural ODE methods (TANGO) for continuous-time modeling.
Synthesize the limitation: all treat facts sequentially or in flat snapshots, missing episodic structure.
citation_hints: Jin et al. (Recurrent Event Network: Autoregressive Structure Inference over Temporal Knowledge Graphs)
Zhu et al. (Learning from History: Modeling Temporal Knowledge Graphs with Sequential Copy-Generation Network)

research paper or technical report introducing 'HiSMatch temporal knowledge graph'
research paper or technical report introducing 'TANGO temporal knowledge graph'

content_bullets: Cover foundational memory architectures: Neural Turing Machines and Differentiable Neural Computer.
Discuss End-to-End Memory Networks and Dynamic Memory Networks for reasoning.
Identify gap: these architectures lack temporal structure in memory organization.
Position EMN as a temporal-specialized memory architecture for KG reasoning.
citation_hints: Graves et al. (Neural Turing Machines)
Graves et al. (Hybrid computing using a neural network with dynamic external memory)
Sukhbaatar et al. (End-To-End Memory Networks)
research paper or technical report introducing 'Dynamic Memory Networks'

content_bullets: Define temporal KG as set of quadruples G = {(e_s, r, e_o, t)}.
Define entity set E, relation set R, and temporal KG completion task.
Formally state the prediction problem: given G_{<=t}, predict (e_s, r, ?, t+1).
Introduce notation: memory matrix M in R^{K x d}, fact embeddings h_i, temporal encodings p_i.
Define the event encoder f(e_s, r, e_o) that maps each temporal fact to a dense embedding.
Combine entity and relation embeddings with a learned composition function.
Describe the embedding dimensionality (200) as specified in experimental_log.md.
citation_hints: Bordes et al. (Translating Embeddings for Modeling Multi-relational Data)

content_bullets: Present the dual temporal encoding: sinusoidal absolute time + learned relative time intervals.
Formally define p_i = p_abs(t_i) + p_rel(delta_t) where delta_t is the time interval.
Explain how this captures both when events occurred and how far apart they are.
citation_hints: Vaswani et al. (Attention Is All You Need)

content_bullets: Describe the temporal clustering algorithm that segments facts into episodes.
Define the content-based addressing mechanism for writing to memory slots.
Formalize the memory write operation: M_k = alpha * M_k + (1 - alpha) * h_episode.
Explain the episode window parameter (7 days) from experimental_log.md.
citation_hints: Graves et al. (Hybrid computing using a neural network with dynamic external memory)

content_bullets: Define the gated attention mechanism for reading from episodic memory.
Formalize: alpha = softmax(q^T * M) where q is the query embedding.
Describe the reasoning head that combines retrieved episode representations with query.
Define the final scoring function for entity prediction.
citation_hints: Sukhbaatar et al. (End-To-End Memory Networks)

content_bullets: Define the training loss: negative log-likelihood over candidate entities.
Describe the AdamW optimizer with learning rate 1e-4 from experimental_log.md.
Specify training procedure: 50 epochs, early stopping on validation MRR.
citation_hints: research paper or technical report introducing 'AdamW optimizer'

content_bullets: Describe the three benchmarks: ICEWS14 (7,128 entities, 72,826 training facts), ICEWS18 (23,033 entities, 373,018 training facts), GDELT (7,691 entities, 2,735,685 training facts) from experimental_log.md.
List evaluation metrics: MRR, Hits@1, Hits@3, Hits@10.
Detail hyperparameters: embedding dim 200, memory slots K=64, episode window 7 days, batch size 1024.
Describe hardware: single NVIDIA A100 GPU.
List all baseline methods: TransE, ConvE, RotatE (static); RE-NET, CyGNet, RE-GCN, HiSMatch, TANGO (temporal).

content_bullets: Present Table 1 with full comparison across all methods on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, GDELT using MRR, Hits@1, Hits@10.
Highlight EMN's 5-8% MRR improvement over TANGO (best prior) on ICEWS14.
Analyze consistent improvements across all three datasets and all metrics.
Discuss Figure 2 (training curves) showing EMN converges faster than baselines.
Present Table 2 comparing wall-clock training time, GPU memory, and parameter count.
Highlight that EMN is competitive with TANGO on efficiency (20.1 vs 22.4 s/epoch).
Analyze sub-linear memory growth: memory slots grow with O(K) not O(|G|).
Present ablation table removing each component: temporal position encoder, episodic grouping, gated attention.
Analyze the relative importance of each component based on MRR and Hits@10 drops.
Highlight that episodic grouping contributes the most (4.5% MRR drop when removed).
Discuss what each ablation variant reveals about the architecture.
Present attention heatmap (Figure 5) showing which memory slots activate for sample queries.
Provide case study: EMN groups temporally related political events (e.g., sanctions, diplomatic meetings, treaties) into coherent episodes on ICEWS14.
Analyze how attention weights reveal the model's temporal reasoning patterns.
Analyze sensitivity to number of memory slots K: test K in {16, 32, 64, 128, 256}.
Analyze sensitivity to episode window size: test {1, 3, 7, 14, 30} days.
Report the trade-off between memory capacity and computational cost.
Restate the contribution: EMN structures temporal KG facts as episodic memory traces for improved reasoning.
Summarize key results: SOTA on 3 benchmarks with interpretable attention.
Acknowledge limitations: assumes discrete timesteps; continuous-time KGs remain unexplored.
Note that the memory write strategy is heuristic-driven (temporal clustering).
Propose extension to continuous-time KGs using neural ODEs for temporal encoding.
Suggest hierarchical episodic memory for multi-granularity temporal reasoning.
Mention potential application to other structured reasoning tasks beyond KG completion.
